title: Small Bowel Obstruction
objectives: Recognize clinical and radiographic features of SBO

guidelineReferences.name: WSES Guidelines on SBO
guidelineReferences.citation: Ten Broek RP, et al. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:29

guidelineReferences.name: EAST Guidelines on SBO
guidelineReferences.citation: Maung AA, et al. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2012;73(5 Suppl 4):S362-9

vignette.text: A 62-year-old woman with history of hysterectomy and appendectomy presents with 2 days of crampy abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and inability to pass gas or stool. She has had 5-6 episodes of bilious vomiting. The pain comes in waves and is periumbilical.

physicalExam.general: Appears uncomfortable, lying still between waves of pain
physicalExam.abdomen: Distended, tympanitic to percussion, high-pitched bowel sounds, diffuse tenderness without peritoneal signs, well-healed surgical scars
physicalExam.hernia: No inguinal or ventral hernias palpable

imaging.XR: Multiple dilated loops of small bowel (>3 cm) with air-fluid levels, minimal colonic gas
imaging.CT: Transition point in mid-ileum with proximal small bowel dilation and decompressed distal bowel, small amount of free fluid, no pneumatosis or portal venous gas, likely adhesive band

questions.stem: The patient presents with crampy pain, vomiting, distension, and obstipation.
questions.question: The classic tetrad of small bowel obstruction is:
options: A. Continuous pain, fever, peritonitis, and shock
B. Colicky pain, vomiting, distension, and obstipation
C. Nausea, diarrhea, weight loss, and anemia
D. Fever, pain, jaundice, and mass
questions.explanation: The classic SBO tetrad: (1) Colicky/crampy abdominal pain (intermittent as bowel contracts against obstruction), (2) Vomiting (earlier and more profuse with proximal obstruction), (3) Abdominal distension, (4) Obstipation (no flatus or stool). This patient has all four.

whyChain.answer: The bowel proximal to obstruction undergoes vigorous contractions trying to push contents past the blockage. This creates waves of pain followed by relief. Continuous severe pain suggests strangulation.

whyChain.why: Why earlier vomiting with proximal obstruction?
whyChain.answer: Less bowel to distend before vomiting reflex triggered. Proximal (jejunal) obstruction causes early, profuse, bilious vomiting. Distal obstruction causes later, more feculent vomiting.

questions.stem: CT shows a transition point with dilated proximal and decompressed distal bowel.
questions.question: The 'transition point' on CT indicates:
options: A. The point where enteral feeding should begin
B. An area of bowel necrosis
C. The junction between jejunum and ileum
D. The site where caliber change indicates the location of obstruction
questions.explanation: The transition point is where dilated proximal bowel (>3 cm for small bowel) meets decompressed distal bowel. This pinpoints the obstruction location and helps identify the cause (adhesion, hernia, mass, stricture). It's the most important CT finding for surgical planning.

whyChain.answer: Accumulated swallowed air and intestinal secretions (8-10 L/day produced proximal to obstruction). The bowel can't absorb or pass this, so it dilates. Normal SB is ≤3 cm diameter.

whyChain.why: What if no transition point is seen?
whyChain.answer: Consider partial obstruction, intermittent obstruction, or ileus rather than mechanical SBO. May need repeat imaging if high clinical suspicion.

questions.stem: The patient has history of previous abdominal surgeries.
questions.question: The most common cause of small bowel obstruction in developed countries is:
options: A. Adhesions from previous surgery
B. Crohn's disease stricture
C. Malignancy
D. Incarcerated hernia
questions.explanation: Adhesions cause 65-75% of SBO in developed countries. Previous surgery is the major risk factor - more extensive surgery = more adhesions. Hernias are second (10-15%), then malignancy (10%). In countries with less surgical access, hernias are more common.

whyChain.answer: Fibrous bands form between bowel loops and abdominal wall after surgery. Bowel can become kinked, compressed, or twisted by these bands, causing mechanical obstruction.
Colorectal, gynecologic, and appendectomy are highest risk. Open surgery causes more than laparoscopic. Lower abdominal surgery causes more than upper.

questions.stem: The labs show Na 132, K 3.2, hypovolemia (BUN/Cr ratio elevated).
questions.question: The electrolyte abnormalities in SBO result from:
options: A. Loss of gastric contents (vomiting) and third-spacing into bowel lumen
B. Inadequate dietary intake alone
C. Increased renal excretion
D. SIADH from pain and stress
questions.explanation: SBO causes massive fluid/electrolyte losses: (1) Vomiting loses gastric fluid (HCl, K+, H2O); (2) Sequestration into dilated bowel lumen (third-spacing); (3) Decreased absorption. Hypokalemic, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis is classic. Dehydration is often severe.

whyChain.answer: The GI tract normally secretes 8-10 L/day that is reabsorbed. In SBO, this is trapped proximally. Patients can sequester several liters into bowel lumen before presentation.
Vomiting loses gastric acid (HCl). Loss of H+ causes alkalosis. Kidney compensates by excreting HCO3- but conserves Na+ (retains HCO3- instead). Volume depletion perpetuates alkalosis.

questions.stem: You are concerned about bowel viability.
questions.question: Strangulation (compromised bowel blood supply) is suggested by all EXCEPT:
options: A. Peritoneal signs on exam
B. Normal lactate level
C. Fever and tachycardia
D. Continuous (non-colicky) severe pain
questions.explanation: EXCEPT normal lactate - all others suggest strangulation. Strangulation signs: continuous severe pain (ischemia), fever, peritoneal signs, tachycardia, shock, elevated lactate, elevated WBC. However, strangulation can exist without elevated lactate - lactate is late and not sensitive.

whyChain.why: Why does pain pattern change with strangulation?
whyChain.answer: Colicky pain becomes continuous because ischemic bowel causes constant peritoneal irritation. 'Cramps turning to constant pain' is a red flag.
Lactate rises late in ischemia (after cell death begins). By the time lactate elevates, necrosis may have occurred. Normal lactate doesn't rule out strangulation.
Decreased bowel wall enhancement, mesenteric haziness/fluid, closed-loop obstruction, bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis intestinalis (late), portal venous gas (late).

questions.stem: This patient has adhesive SBO without signs of strangulation.
questions.question: Initial management should include:
options: A. NPO, nasogastric tube, IV fluid resuscitation, serial exams
B. Colonoscopy to decompress
C. Clear liquid diet and observation
D. Immediate surgery
questions.explanation: Uncomplicated adhesive SBO can often be managed non-operatively: NPO, NG decompression, aggressive IV fluids, electrolyte correction, and serial abdominal exams. 65-80% of adhesive SBO resolves without surgery. Key: frequent reassessment for strangulation signs.

whyChain.answer: Decompresses the dilated proximal bowel, reduces vomiting/aspiration risk, may reduce bowel distension enough for obstruction to resolve, and allows monitoring of output.

whyChain.why: How long to trial non-operative management?
whyChain.answer: Generally 24-72 hours if patient stable and improving. If no resolution by 72 hours, surgery likely needed. Gastrografin can be diagnostic and therapeutic.

questions.stem: You are considering water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) challenge.
questions.question: Gastrografin in adhesive SBO:
options: A. Replaces need for CT imaging
B. Can predict need for surgery (contrast in colon by 24h = resolution) and may be therapeutic
C. Is contraindicated due to aspiration risk
D. Should only be given after 5 days of non-operative management
questions.explanation: Gastrografin has dual benefit: (1) Diagnostic - contrast in colon by 4-24 hours predicts resolution without surgery (97% PPV); (2) Therapeutic - hyperosmolar contrast draws fluid into lumen, may reduce edema and help adhesion-related obstruction resolve. Meta-analyses support its use.

whyChain.answer: After initial resuscitation and NG decompression, typically at 24-48 hours if not improving. Follow with X-ray at 4-24 hours. Contrast in colon = will resolve; no contrast = likely needs surgery.

Avoid if complete obstruction with suspected strangulation (needs surgery, not contrast). Avoid aspiration - ensure adequate NG decompression. Use with caution if perforation suspected (water-soluble is safer than barium).

questions.stem: On day 2, the patient develops worsening pain that is now constant, fever 101.5°F, and periumbilical tenderness with guarding.
questions.question: The most appropriate next step is:
options: A. Repeat CT scan
B. Start TPN for nutritional support
C. Continue observation for another 24 hours
D. Urgent surgical exploration
questions.explanation: The development of peritoneal signs (guarding, rebound), continuous pain (replacing colicky pain), and fever suggests strangulation/ischemia. This is a surgical emergency - do not delay for more imaging. Time is bowel - dead bowel means resection, complications, potential death.

whyChain.why: What are absolute indications for surgery?
whyChain.answer: Peritonitis, strangulation signs (fever, continuous pain, tachycardia), complete obstruction not resolving, closed-loop obstruction on CT, or clinical deterioration. Don't wait for lactate to rise.

Exploratory laparotomy (or laparoscopy in selected cases), lysis of adhesions, assessment of bowel viability. Necrotic bowel is resected. Primary anastomosis if viable; may need stoma if contamination/instability.

questions.stem: The patient underwent successful adhesiolysis and recovered well.
questions.question: To reduce future adhesion formation and SBO recurrence, which intervention has the most evidence?
options: A. Adhesion barrier placement during surgery
B. Nasogastric tube for 5 days postop
C. Early postoperative ambulation
D. Prophylactic antibiotics for 2 weeks
questions.explanation: Adhesion barriers (hyaluronic acid-carboxymethylcellulose membrane) reduce adhesion formation and decrease risk of reoperation for SBO. Meta-analyses support their use in high-risk surgeries. Other strategies (minimally invasive surgery, meticulous technique) also help but have less robust evidence.

whyChain.answer: They create a physical separation between bowel and peritoneum during healing when adhesions form (first 7 days). The barrier is absorbed after this critical period.
Laparoscopic surgery causes fewer adhesions than open surgery due to less tissue trauma, less peritoneal drying, and less foreign body reaction. Preferred when feasible.

questions.stem: You are distinguishing mechanical SBO from paralytic ileus.
questions.question: Which finding favors mechanical SBO over ileus?
options: A. Transition point on CT with distal decompression
B. Diffuse abdominal distension without tenderness
C. Absent bowel sounds
D. Dilated small and large bowel on imaging
questions.explanation: A discrete transition point with proximal dilation and distal decompression = mechanical obstruction. Ileus shows diffuse dilation of small AND large bowel without transition point. Bowel sounds are often hyperactive (high-pitched, tinkling) in early SBO but absent in ileus.

whyChain.answer: Post-operative (most common), electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia), medications (opioids), peritonitis, retroperitoneal processes, systemic illness. The bowel stops peristalsis but isn't mechanically blocked.
Ileus is managed by treating underlying cause - no surgery needed. Mechanical SBO may need surgery. Wrong diagnosis leads to wrong treatment.

questions.stem: Integrating the approach to this patient...
questions.question: The key decision in SBO management is distinguishing:
options: A. Proximal vs distal obstruction
B. Simple (non-strangulated) vs complicated (strangulated) SBO
C. First episode vs recurrent SBO
D. Complete vs incomplete obstruction
questions.explanation: The critical distinction is simple (uncomplicated) vs strangulated (complicated) SBO. Simple SBO can be managed non-operatively with high success rates. Strangulated SBO requires URGENT surgery to prevent bowel necrosis and death. Every clinical decision aims to identify or exclude strangulation.

whyChain.why: What drives the urgency of this distinction?
whyChain.answer: Strangulated bowel = ischemia = necrosis within hours. Necrotic bowel leads to perforation, peritonitis, sepsis, and death. Time from strangulation onset to surgery determines outcome.

whyChain.why: How does this guide serial exams?
whyChain.answer: Non-operative patients need frequent reassessment (q4-6h) for conversion to strangulation. Any worsening = surgical consultation. The goal is early detection before necrosis occurs.
